section.title: God's Field and God's Temple
section.sectionRationale: Having shown that the gospel is God's wisdom received by the Spirit, Paul now diagnoses the Corinthians' factionalism as spiritual infancy. Their boasting in Paul or Apollos treats the servants as if they were the source. Paul overturns this with three images: the church is God's field where He alone gives the growth, God's building whose one foundation is Jesus Christ, and God's temple where the Spirit dwells. Every worker's labour will be tested by fire, and boasting in men is exposed as folly, for all things already belong to those who belong to Christ.
section.thesis: The quarrelling Corinthians are still worldly, but their leaders are only servants through whom God works; the church is God's field, God's building on the one foundation of Jesus Christ, and God's holy temple indwelt by the Spirit, so no one should boast in men when all things are already theirs in Christ.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the Corinthians still worldly and quarrelling; God's field where He alone gives the growth; the one foundation of Christ, the building tested by fire, and the temple of the Spirit; and the folly of boasting in men when all things are yours in Christ.

units.label: Still infants
units.note: worldly, marked by jealousy and dissension

verses.text: Brothers, I could not address you as spiritual, but as worldly, as {{nepios|infants}} in Christ.
I gave you milk, not solid food, for you were not yet ready for solid food. In fact, you are still not ready,
for you are still worldly. For since there is jealousy and dissension among you, are you not worldly? Are you not walking in the way of man?
For when one of you says, “I follow Paul,” and another, “I follow Apollos,” are you not mere men?

units.label: God's field
units.note: servants plant and water, but God gives the growth

verses.text: What then is Apollos? And what is Paul? They are {{diakonos|servants}} through whom you believed, as the Lord has assigned to each his role.
I planted the seed and Apollos watered it, but God made it grow.
So neither he who plants nor he who waters is anything, but only God, who makes things grow.
He who plants and he who waters are one in purpose, and each will be rewarded according to his own labor.
For we are God’s fellow workers; you are God’s field, God’s building.

units.label: The one foundation and God's temple
units.note: Christ the foundation, the work tested by fire, the Spirit's dwelling

verses.text: By the grace God has given me, I laid a foundation as an expert builder, and someone else is building on it. But each one must be careful how he builds.
For no one can lay a {{themelios|foundation}} other than the one already laid, which is Jesus Christ.
If anyone builds on this foundation using gold, silver, precious stones, wood, hay, or straw,
his workmanship will be evident, because the Day will bring it to light. It will be revealed with fire, and the fire will prove the quality of each man’s work.
If what he has built survives, he will receive a reward.
If it is burned up, he will suffer loss. He himself will be saved, but only as if through the flames.
Do you not know that you yourselves are God’s {{naos|temple}}, and that God’s Spirit dwells in you?
If anyone destroys God’s temple, God will destroy him; for God’s temple is holy, and you are that temple.
Let no one deceive himself. If any of you thinks he is wise in this age, he should become a fool, so that he may become wise.
For the wisdom of this world is foolishness in God’s sight. As it is written: “He catches the wise in their craftiness.”
And again, “The Lord knows that the thoughts of the wise are futile.”
Therefore, stop boasting in men. All things are yours,
whether Paul or Apollos or Cephas or the world or life or death or the present or the future. All of them belong to you,
and you belong to Christ, and Christ belongs to God.

nepios.gloss: An infant, a small child, one not yet able to speak or take solid food. Paul calls the Corinthians infants in Christ, not to deny their conversion but to expose their immaturity: their jealousy and party spirit show they are still feeding on milk when they should be growing toward maturity in Christ.

diakonos.gloss: A servant, minister, or attendant who carries out the will of another. Paul and Apollos are not rival masters to be followed but diakonoi, waiters at another's table, through whom the Corinthians came to believe. The word deflates all boasting in leaders: the servant is nothing, and God who gives the growth is everything.

themelios.gloss: A foundation, the base on which a building rests. There is only one foundation for the church, already laid, which is Jesus Christ Himself. No teacher may lay another; every builder must build on Him, and the value of each one's work will be tested against this fixed and finished foundation.

naos.gloss: The temple, specifically the inner sanctuary where God dwells, not merely the temple precincts. Astonishingly, Paul says the church together is God's naos and the Spirit dwells in it. What was once the holy of holies in Jerusalem is now the gathered people of God, holy and not to be destroyed by division.

basic.context: The Corinthians were splitting into fan clubs, 'I follow Paul,' 'I follow Apollos.' Paul says this proves they are still spiritual babies. He then reframes the whole thing with pictures: the church is a field, and God alone makes the seed grow; the church is a building, and its only foundation is Jesus; the church is God's temple, where His Spirit lives. Leaders are just servants and workers. One day every worker's labour will be tested by fire. So stop boasting in men, because in Christ everything already belongs to you.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul argues by images rather than abstract rules: field, building, temple. Each picture makes the same point from a different angle, that God is the true owner and worker, and the leaders are only His servants. The passage moves from diagnosis, you are worldly, to correction, God gives the growth, to warning, your work will be tested, to application, so stop boasting.
basic.originalLanguages: The Corinthians are nepioi, infants, still on milk. Paul and Apollos are diakonoi, servants who wait at God's table, not masters to follow. The church rests on one themelios, foundation, Jesus Christ, and no other can be laid. And the church together is God's naos, the inner sanctuary, where the Spirit dwells, so to wreck the church by division is to desecrate God's temple.
basic.typology: The temple where God dwelt among Israel now finds its fulfilment in the church, the living temple indwelt by the Spirit. The testing fire recalls the refining fire of God's judgment throughout Scripture, which purifies what is genuine and consumes what is worthless.
basic.christ: Jesus Christ is the one foundation, already laid, on which the whole church is built; no rival foundation can be set beside Him. He is the ground of all ministry and the one to whom the church belongs: you belong to Christ, and Christ belongs to God. All the treasures the Corinthians grasped at through their favourite teachers are already theirs because they are His.
basic.god: God is the true worker and owner: He gives the growth, He owns the field, the building, and the temple, and He dwells in His people by the Spirit. He is holy, so His temple is holy and must not be destroyed. He is just, testing each worker's labour by fire, and gracious, saving even the one whose work is burned up.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians 2 says the church is built on the apostles and prophets with Christ as cornerstone. First Peter 2 calls believers living stones built into a spiritual house. Second Corinthians 6 says we are the temple of the living God. Isaiah 28 speaks of the tested cornerstone God lays in Zion.
basic.oneStory: God has always sought to dwell with His people, in Eden, the tabernacle, and the temple. Here that story reaches a new stage: through Christ the one foundation, God now makes His people themselves the temple where His Spirit lives. The whole Bible moves toward God dwelling with humanity, fulfilled in Christ and consummated when God dwells with His people forever.

context.literary: This chapter continues the argument begun in 1:10 against the divisions in the church. Paul returns from the wisdom of the Spirit in chapter 2 to the concrete problem: their party spirit. He builds the section around three metaphors, field in verses 5 to 9, building in verses 10 to 15, and temple in verses 16 to 17, then closes with a sweeping 'all things are yours' that dissolves the whole basis for boasting.
context.historical: Corinth prized rhetoric and celebrity teachers, and clients attached themselves to patrons in loyal factions. The believers imported this culture into the church, ranking Paul, Apollos, and Cephas as competing figureheads. Paul's images of servant, farmer, and builder cut against a status culture that measured worth by eloquence and following.
context.canonical: The temple theme reaches back to the tabernacle and Solomon's temple and forward to the new creation where God dwells with His people. The image of the church as God's building and temple is developed in Ephesians 2:19-22 and 1 Peter 2:4-8, and the tested foundation echoes Isaiah 28:16, quoted of Christ in the New Testament.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To heal the Corinthian factions by showing that leaders are mere servants and God alone gives the growth, that Christ is the one foundation on which every worker builds and by which every work is tested, and that the church is God's holy temple, so that boasting in men is both foolish and dangerous.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The images of field, building, and temple describe what the church truly is; the commands, stop boasting in men and be careful how you build, are the prescriptions that follow. The warning about work tested by fire is a sober truth for all who serve, not a licence for carelessness.

debates.question: What does it mean that the worker is 'saved, but only as if through the flames' in verse 15?

views.label: A true believer whose ministry proves worthless
views.case: The person is genuinely saved yet loses the reward for shoddy work; he escapes like a man running from a burning house with nothing but his life. Salvation is by grace, but the quality of service is really assessed.

views.label: A picture of loss without a second-chance purgatory
views.case: The verse is sometimes read to support a purifying state after death, but the text speaks of the worker's work being burned, not the worker being purged; the man himself is simply saved, though empty-handed.

debates.bearing: The orthodox reading affirms assurance of salvation by grace while taking seriously that our labour for Christ will be tested; it does not teach salvation by works or a purgatory that adds to the finished work of Christ.

study.originalLanguages: Paul's diagnosis is blunt: the Corinthians are nepioi, infants, still drinking milk. His correction turns on the word diakonos, servant: Paul and Apollos are only waiters through whom the Corinthians believed, while God gives the growth. The building image rests on themelios, the one foundation already laid, Jesus Christ, which no builder may replace. And the climax names the church God's naos, the inner sanctuary where God dwells, so that splitting the church is not a small matter of preference but the desecration of God's temple, which He will judge.

typology.fulfillment: The tabernacle and Jerusalem temple, God's dwelling among Israel, are fulfilled in the church, the living temple indwelt by the Spirit, and finally in the new creation where God dwells with His people forever.
The sure foundation God lays in Zion, Isaiah 28:16, is fulfilled in Jesus Christ, the one foundation on which the church is built and by which every work is proved.

study.christ: Christ stands at the centre as the one foundation of the church, already laid, on which every faithful worker must build and against which every ministry is measured. He is not one option among the teachers the Corinthians prized but the ground of the whole building. The chapter ends by locating everything in Him: all things, Paul, Apollos, Cephas, the world, life, death, present, and future, belong to those who belong to Christ, and Christ belongs to God. To have Christ is to have everything, which is why boasting in mere men is such folly.
study.god: God is the true worker and owner throughout. He gives the growth that no planter or waterer can produce. He owns the field, raises the building, and dwells in the temple by His Spirit. He is holy, so His temple is holy and must not be destroyed by division, and He is just, testing each worker's labour by fire so that genuine service is rewarded and worthless work consumed. Yet even the one whose work is burned up is saved, a mark of His grace.

crossReferences.note: The church built on the apostles and prophets with Christ as cornerstone, growing into a holy temple in the Lord.
Believers as living stones built into a spiritual house, with Christ the chosen and precious cornerstone.
We are the temple of the living God, echoing the temple image applied to the church here.
God lays in Zion a tested and precious cornerstone, fulfilled in Christ the one foundation.
The Lord will bring to light what is hidden and expose the heart, matching the Day that tests each worker's labour.

study.oneStory: From Eden onward God has sought to dwell with His people, in the tabernacle and the temple, His presence among them. Here the story advances: through Christ the one foundation, God makes His people themselves the temple where His Spirit lives. The factions at Corinth would fracture the very dwelling of God, but the gospel gathers a people into one building whose owner, worker, and treasure is God Himself. The whole Bible runs toward God dwelling with humanity, secured in Christ and completed in the new creation.

quiz.q: Why does Paul call the Corinthians infants in Christ?

options.text: Their jealousy and party spirit show they are still spiritually immature.
options.note: Verse 3 links their worldliness to jealousy and dissension.

options.text: They had only recently been converted and needed time.
options.note: Newness plays a part, but Paul's point is their persisting immaturity shown in strife.

options.text: They lacked the intelligence to grasp deep teaching.
options.note: The issue is not intellect but worldliness and division.

options.text: They were never truly converted at all.
options.note: Paul still calls them brothers in Christ, though immature.

quiz.q: What is Paul's main point in the image of planting and watering?

options.text: Leaders are only servants; God alone gives the growth.
options.note: Verses 6 and 7 make God the one who makes things grow.

options.text: Paul deserves more honour because he planted first.
options.note: The opposite; neither planter nor waterer is anything before God.

options.text: The Corinthians should choose the best teacher to follow.
options.note: The whole point undercuts choosing sides among servants.

options.text: Church growth depends entirely on human effort.
options.note: Paul says only God, who makes things grow, is anything.

quiz.q: What is the one foundation of the church?

options.text: Jesus Christ, the only foundation that can be laid.
options.note: Verse 11 says no other foundation can be laid than Jesus Christ.

options.text: The teaching of the apostles as a whole.
options.note: The apostles build, but the foundation itself is Christ.

options.text: The unity and good works of the congregation.
options.note: These matter, but they are not the foundation, which is Christ alone.

options.text: Whichever leader a believer chooses to follow.
options.note: This is precisely the error Paul is correcting.

quiz.q: What does it mean that the church is God's temple?

options.text: God's Spirit dwells in the gathered people, who are holy and must not be destroyed.
options.note: Verses 16 and 17 make the church the dwelling of the Spirit.

options.text: The church building itself is sacred space.
options.note: The temple here is the people, not a physical structure.

options.text: Only especially devout members are the temple.
options.note: Paul addresses the whole church, you yourselves, as the temple.

options.text: The temple language is only a loose figure of speech.
options.note: Paul treats it seriously: destroying this temple brings God's judgment.

quiz.q: Why should the Corinthians stop boasting in men?

options.text: Because all things are already theirs, and they belong to Christ.
options.note: Verses 21 to 23 dissolve boasting: all things are yours, and you are Christ's.

options.text: Because boasting is simply bad manners.
options.note: True enough, but Paul's reason is far deeper: everything is already theirs in Christ.

options.text: Because only Paul deserves their loyalty.
options.note: Paul refuses such loyalty; he is only a servant.

options.text: Because leaders do not matter to the church at all.
options.note: Leaders matter as servants; the point is not to boast in them.

questions: Paul measures maturity not by knowledge but by whether jealousy and division mark our life. Where might that same test expose immaturity in you?
God alone gives the growth. How does that free you from both pride when ministry flourishes and despair when it seems fruitless?
There is only one foundation, Jesus Christ. What are you tempted to build your life or your church upon instead of Him?
Your work for Christ will one day be tested by fire. What would it change this week to build with gold rather than straw?
You are God's temple, the dwelling of His Spirit. How does knowing that the Spirit lives in the gathered church shape the way you treat other believers?
All things are yours, and you belong to Christ. Where are you grasping for something that is already yours in Him?

translationNotes.note: The phrase rendered God's fellow workers means workers who belong to God and labour in His service, not workers on the same level as God; the surrounding verses stress that God alone gives the growth.

Saved, but only as if through the flames pictures a man escaping a burning house with his life but nothing else; it affirms salvation by grace while the worthless work is lost, and does not teach a purgatory.
You yourselves are God's temple uses a plural you with a singular temple: the whole gathered church together is the one dwelling of the Spirit, not merely each believer individually.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 3:1-23; any em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas or semicolons per the no-dash style rule, with wording unchanged.
